Welcome back to Dry Powder. I=E2=80=99m Bill Cohan.

=C2=A0

In the several decades I=E2=80=99ve spent around private equity firms=E2=80=
=94first as a banker, and then as a journalist covering them=E2=80=94I have=
 never come across a legal ruling as scathing as the one issued early last =
month by a Delaware judge against VitalCaring Group, a private equity-backe=
d health and hospice care provider. Of course, competition among P.E. firms=
 has never been as ruthless as it is today, and this
lawsuit, which is the subject of today=E2=80=99s issue, captures just how c=
utthroat the industry has become.

But first, here=E2=80=99s Marion Maneker with the St. Barts N.Y.E. roll cal=
l=E2=80=A6

Marion Maneker
Marion Maneker

* St. Barts=E2=80=99 billionaire boat show: The holiday break always sends =
denizens of the art world scattering to a variety of destinations, whether =
humble or remote and exotic. On Instagram, I spotted some British art deale=
rs and their families in France. Another American dealer, whose wife has he=
r own gallery with partners, started in Paris before decamping to Morocco. =
Meanwhile, half the world seemed to be in Japan this year, due to the cheap=
 yen against the dollar, including some
collectors I know.=C2=A0

But the New Year=E2=80=99s place to be for the absurdly rich was, as is oft=
en the case, St. Barts. Looking at the AIS data=E2=80=94yachting=E2=80=99s =
version of tracking the tail numbers on private jets=E2=80=94I could see a =
number of boat owners there who range from dabbling buyers to serious art c=

Geffen, who may be one of the most successful collectors of the last 50 yea=
rs. (If Geffen himself wasn=E2=80=99t there, his boat Rising Sun certainly =
was.) Bernard Arnault=E2=80=99s Symphony was anchored off Gustavia, too, no=
t far from Koru, owned by Jeff Bezos, one of the few billionaires in Arnaul=
t=E2=80=99s wealth class, whose name everyone mentions after Ken Griffin=E2=
=80=99s when there=E2=80=99s a really
big-ticket artwork on offer.

Other collecting yachties worth noting: Eyal Ofer, of the Ofer family of ma=
jor collectors=E2=80=94whose patriarch, Sammy Ofer, briefly partnered with =

A talmudic reading of the underappreciated saga surrounding
VitalCaring Group, a private equity concoction that a Delaware judge has br=
anded a hallmark of modern duplicity.

William D. Cohan ( https://puck.news/author/william-cohan/?utm_campaign=3DD=
ry+Powder+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F5%2F25%29&utm_content=3DDry+Powder+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%2812%2F29%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&=
utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )
William D. Cohan ( https://puck.news/author/william-cohan/?utm_campaign=3DD=
ry+Powder+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%281%2F5%2F25%29&utm_content=3DDry+Powder+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%2812%2F29%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&=
utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )

Private equity has become such an astoundingly lucrative
business that I guess it is no surprise that the temptation of ever greater=
 fortune sometimes leads its practitioners astray. On December 4, the Delaw=
are Court of Chancery=E2=80=99s Judge Lori Will wrote a notably damning opi=
nion involving VitalCaring Group, which had been created by two mid-market =
P.E. firms=E2=80=94The Vistria Group, based in Chicago, and Nautic Partners=
, based in Providence=E2=80=94to buy companies in the home healthcare indus=
try. According to the judge, Vistria and Nautic aided and
abetted the =E2=80=9Cegregious breaches of the duty of loyalty=E2=80=9D owe=
d by three executives that the firms had poached from their former employer=
, Encompass Health Corporation.

=C2=A0

I=E2=80=99ve read many corporate legal opinions over the decades, but I can=
=E2=80=99t remember one as scathing as Judge Will=E2=80=99s 114-page barn b=
urner of a ruling in a lawsuit brought by Encompass against Vistria and its=
 senior partner David
Schuppan, and Nautic and its managing director, Christopher Corey, among ot=
hers. =E2=80=9CDelaware law demands that corporate officers act with the ut=
most loyalty to the entity they serve,=E2=80=9D she wrote. =E2=80=9CThey mu=
st avoid advantaging themselves at the corporation=E2=80=99s expense. They =
cannot compete with the corporation or divert corporate opportunities from =
it without its consent. And they must undertake good faith efforts to advan=
ce the corporation=E2=80=99s best interests. The former officers
at issue here lost sight of this enduring duty.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

Of course, competition between private equity firms has never been so inten=
se. Over the past three decades, the number of firms competing for deals ha=
s risen exponentially=E2=80=94along with the trillions of dollars they coll=
ectively manage. We=E2=80=99ve all heard of the biggies=E2=80=94Blackstone,=
 Carlyle, Apollo (the subject of my new book), KKR, and TPG (an investor in
Puck), among others ranking in the top 50. According to IBISWorld, however,=
 there are now more than 11,000 private equity and hedge fund firms=E2=80=
=94alts, in the lingua franca of modern finance=E2=80=94operating in the Un=
ited States alone. According to ProPublica, they collectively manage more t=
han $6 trillion in assets=E2=80=94up from less than $600 billion in 2000.=
=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Until 2004, when I left
JPMorgan Chase, I worked as a banker to private equity firms when the indus=
try was still in its relative infancy. I=E2=80=99ve been reporting and writ=
ing about these firms ever since. Before reading Judge Will=E2=80=99s damni=
ng ruling, I=E2=80=99d never heard of either Vistria or Nautic, which goes =
to show how flooded the zone has become. The only dealmaker I recognized at=
 either firm was Deval Patrick, the former good-guy governor of Massachuset=
ts, who worked at Bain Capital before he joined Vistria.
But the backstory of the two firms=E2=80=99 legal troubles=E2=80=94the psyc=
hodrama of building and selling companies, and the ego-fueled attempts to b=
uy them back=E2=80=94will be familiar to many of my readers.

=C2=A0

The sorry saga began when April Anthony, the founder and C.E.O. of what was=
 then called Encompass Home Health & Hospice, a home healthcare company, =
=E2=80=9Cbecame disillusioned after her business was bought by a
large public healthcare company=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94the Birmingham, Alabama-ba=
sed HealthSouth=E2=80=94in 2014. After Anthony=E2=80=99s initial attempts t=
o buy back Encompass failed, she recruited two of her colleagues=E2=80=94pr=
esident Luke James and C.F.O. Chris Walker=E2=80=94and =E2=80=9Csecretly pa=
rtnered=E2=80=9D with Vistria and Nautic to =E2=80=9Cforge another path=E2=
=80=9D that got them all in a whole heap of trouble. Together, the trio for=
med a new entity=E2=80=94what became VitalCaring=E2=80=94to buy companies t=
hat
competed with Encompass, all while mostly still working for Encompass, in v=
iolation of their contractual obligations to the company. Per the ruling, =
=E2=80=9CAnthony and her co-venturers identified three acquisition targets =
to form the base of their enterprise. Their scheme was kept from Encompass =
and its private equity investors. They took opportunities, resources, and i=
nformation belonging to Encompass to set themselves up for success. After t=
he new company was formed, Anthony
induced more Encompass employees to join her. Anthony=E2=80=99s private equ=
ity partners were active participants in the fiduciaries=E2=80=99 misconduc=
t.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

According to Judge Will, this group =E2=80=9Cundertook stunning efforts to =
conceal their actions=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94including exchanging documents throu=
gh lawyers and on a golf course, and using sophomoric code names like =E2=
=80=9CVoldemort,=E2=80=9D a veiled reference to Anthony, since, you know, s=
he could not
be named. A phony employee-recruitment process was contrived to set up Vita=
lCaring, with Anthony operating as C.E.O. despite her official ongoing obli=
gations at Encompass, referred to as EHC in the court documents. Meanwhile,=
 various records demonstrating her involvement were deleted or manipulated.=
=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The whole thing might have worked out just fine but for the fact that one E=
HC employee Anthony was trying to recruit
later changed her mind and told the new C.E.O. of EHC what was going on.

Too Much Is Never Enough
------------------------

It=E2=80=99s a little late for a holiday season morality tale, but it=E2=80=
=99s
worth noting that no one in this saga was at risk of going hungry. Accordin=
g to a lavish 2020 Forbes profile, Anthony drives around town in a plum-col=
ored Bentley, a black Rolls-Royce Wraith, and a white McLaren, and has rank=
ed as high as No. 45 on the magazine=E2=80=99s list of America=E2=80=99s Ri=
chest Self-Made Women, tied with fellow Houston native Beyonc=C3=A9. Her en=
trepreneurial career began in the 1990s with Liberty Health Services, a hom=
e healthcare business with about 25 employees
and 50 in-home patients. She sold that company for $40 million in 1996, and=
 in 1998 she took the $3 million she received in proceeds=E2=80=94and the l=
essons that she had learned in the salt mines of entrepreneurship=E2=80=94t=
o form Encompass Home Health & Hospice by rolling up some 17 struggling Tex=
as home healthcare providers. In 2004, she sold two-thirds of Encompass to =
the buyout firm Cressey & Company at a valuation of $280 million. She also =
created Homecare Homebase, a medical records software
company.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In 2014, Anthony demonstrated that she had mastered the dark arts of the pr=
ivate equity business. Along with Cressey, she sold Encompass for $750 mill=
ion to the HealthSouth Corporation, which later changed its name to Encompa=
ss Health Corporation. Anthony received $70 million in proceeds from that s=
ale, $53 million of which she rolled into the equity of the new company. Sh=
e remained C.E.O.
of the home health and hospice division of Encompass Health Corporation.

=C2=A0

With Anthony at the helm and James as president, Encompass=E2=80=99s home h=
ealth and hospice division grew its revenue from $400 million in 2014 to $1=
=2E1 billion in 2021, receiving a big b=
oost from the demand for its services=20=
during the pandemic. It also made some 36 acquisitions, totaling $750 milli=
on. In the spring of 2020, Anthony and James
sold the rest of the equity they=E2=80=99d rolled over into Encompass, for =
$370 million and $45 million, respectively. That year, they told Forbes the=
y were =E2=80=9Cready to move on=E2=80=9D from Encompass Health and wanted =
to focus on end-of-life care.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

About a month later, however, Christopher Corey, at Nautic, approached Anth=
ony, whom he had known for more than a decade, and suggested they work toge=
ther again.
According to Judge Will=E2=80=99s ruling, Anthony told Corey that she wante=
d to leave EHC and start a new company. But first, according to the documen=
t, she wanted to see if she could buy her old company=E2=80=94the home and =
hospice care division=E2=80=94back from Encompass.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Anthony and Corey worked together to model how the acquisition might work. =
In September 2020, Anthony also contacted David Schuppan, a former Bear Ste=
arns
banker whom she knew from when he worked at Cressey & Company, and who was =
now a senior partner at Vistria and co-head of its healthcare business. Ant=
hony introduced Corey to Schuppan, and the two buyout firms agreed to work =
together with her on the project. Later that month, Anthony approached the =
C.E.O. of Encompass Health with her proposal.=C2=A0

What Could Possibly Go Wrong?
-----------------------------

In the midst of those discussions, Anthony also made a bid on
behalf of Encompass to acquire a new entity, the home health and hospice se=
rvices business of a company called Brookdale, for between $350 and $400 mi=
llion. She also told Nautic and Vistria about the Brookdale bid without tel=
ling her bosses at Encompass that she had shared that information with her =
two buyout firm partners.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Meanwhile, Nautic and Vistria began studying Brookdale=E2=80=99s business. =
In
December 2020, Nautic submitted its own bid for the Brookdale division, whi=
le Vistria approached Encompass about bidding jointly for the same property=
=2E =E2=80=9CCorey sent a Nautic dec=
k to Anthony=E2=80=99s husband=E2=80=99s=20=
personal email modeling a combined leveraged buyout of Encompass Home Healt=
h and an acquisition of Brookdale=E2=80=99s home health business,=E2=80=9D =
the judge wrote. =E2=80=9CThe deck was then forwarded to Walker=E2=80=99s p=
ersonal email, with the subject line changed to =E2=80=98From Mark Anthony=
=E2=80=99 (Anthony=E2=80=99s husband). Anthony sought
input from Walker and James to refine the projections using Encompass=E2=80=
=99s information.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

Encompass ended up passing on the Brookdale property. That=E2=80=99s when A=
nthony, Nautic, and Vistria submitted a non-binding bid to buy Anthony=E2=
=80=99s old business back from Encompass for $3.6 billion. =E2=80=9CThe pro=
posal cautioned that =E2=80=98April [was] not likely to support an alternat=
ive transaction involving the sale of [Encompass Home
Health] to another third party or a public offering of the [Encompass Home =
Health] business in a spin-out transaction,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Judge Will wr=
ote. =E2=80=9CAnthony planned to resign if EHC rejected it. EHC was surpris=
ed. Before receiving the proposal, EHC had no idea that Anthony was partner=
ing with private equity firms on a transaction involving Encompass Home Hea=
lth.=E2=80=9D EHC never responded to the buyout proposal, and it expired af=
ter 30 days.

=C2=A0

In January 2021, Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria tried another tack. They deci=
ded to set up a new company, in which they would all invest, to buy other h=
ome and hospice care companies to compete with Encompass=E2=80=94even thoug=
h Anthony remained the C.E.O. of a division of Encompass. That=E2=80=99s a =
Wall Street no-no.

=C2=A0

They hired legal advisors at Ropes & Gray, the august Boston firm, and enga=
ged
Harris Williams, the middle-market investment bank, to help find acquisitio=
n targets for the new company. The group quickly identified three prospects=
: Homecare Holdings, which Harris Williams was selling; Vital Health Care; =
and Kare-In-Home. Anthony was heavily involved in the creation of the new c=
ompany, according to Judge Will, and the negotiation of the acquisition tar=
gets while still at EHC. =E2=80=9CShe had frequent Zoom meetings, phone cal=
ls, and in-person meetings with Nautic, Vistria, and
representatives from the target companies,=E2=80=9D the judge wrote. =E2=80=
=9CReferences to her involvement were scrubbed or replaced with sly codenam=
es.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Anthony mentioned none of this to her bosses at Encompass. =E2=80=9CIt was =
not lost on Nautic and Vistria that Anthony=E2=80=99s fiduciary duties to E=
ncompass created risks to their plans,=E2=80=9D the judge wrote. =E2=80=9CT=
hey were also concerned about contractual restrictive covenants that would =
continue
to bind Anthony after her employment ended. They asked Ropes for advice on =
navigating these issues.=E2=80=9D Ropes provided the advice, which, accordi=
ng to Judge Will, the defendants ignored.=C2=A0

43 Percent
----------

On March 18, 2021, Anthony informed Encompass that she would be
resigning, effective June 18. She said that she planned to spend time at he=
r homes in Cabo and Idaho, and playing golf. =E2=80=9CThat was false,=E2=80=
=9D the judge wrote. =E2=80=9CAnthony instead continued her efforts to form=
 a home healthcare company with Nautic and Vistria. Anthony=E2=80=99s effor=
ts included recruiting certain Encompass employees for the venture.=E2=80=
=9D=C2=A0

=C2=A0

On July 30, 2021, the new company bought Homecare Holdings for
$192 million. In August 2021, it bought Vital for $106 million. In December=
 2021, it bought Kare for $110 million. Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria each i=
nvested $87 million for a one-third stake in VitalCaring Group.

=C2=A0

In September 2021, after an EHC employee told the new EHC C.E.O. about Anth=
ony=E2=80=99s recruitment efforts, EHC sent cease and desist letters to Ant=
hony, Nautic, and Vistria, among others. The next
month, EHC sued Anthony in Texas state court for breaching the restrictive =
covenants in her EHC contract. While the Texas court ruled that Anthony had=
 breached some of the covenants, it awarded no damages to EHC but required =
Anthony to comply with her contractual covenants until her contract expired=
=2E The court found that Nautic and=20=
Vistria =E2=80=9Chad tried to conceal Ant=
hony=E2=80=99s involvement.=E2=80=9D Each side paid their own legal fees. A=
nthony became the C.E.O. of VitalCaring in August 2022, after the covenant
restrictions lapsed.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

A month later, EHC and several affiliates sued Nautic, Vistria, Corey, Schu=
ppan, and Walker in Delaware court, alleging six counts of aiding and abett=
ing Anthony in breaches of her fiduciary duty to EHC, among other charges. =
A seven-day trial was held in December 2023. In her ruling a year later, Ju=
dge Will has thrown pretty much the whole book at Nautic and Vistria. Accor=
ding to
the Encompass press release, =E2=80=9CAfter considering =E2=80=98the damnin=
g record presented at trial,=E2=80=99 the Court found that, while employed =
by Encompass Health, Anthony, James, and Walker usurped acquisition opportu=
nities falling within Encompass Health=E2=80=99s line of business, used Enc=
ompass Health=E2=80=99s confidential information, and swayed key Encompass =
Health employees to join them with the promise of equity in the home health=
 and hospice competitor that Anthony now heads.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

Judge Will was more than a little peeved. =E2=80=9CThe evidence establishes=
 that Anthony and James strove to benefit themselves at Encompass=E2=80=99s=
 expense,=E2=80=9D she wrote. =E2=80=9CThey did so willfully, using code na=
mes and secretly exchanging diligence materials to hide their misconduct. T=
his deception is unexcused by the defendants=E2=80=99 post hoc justificatio=
ns.=E2=80=9D She added, =E2=80=9CDisillusioned with EHC, Anthony set out to=
 =E2=80=98get [her] baby back.=E2=80=99 She partnered with Nautic and
Vistria and brought along James and Walker. When her plan failed, she turne=
d her focus to creating a competitor. As she told Nautic=E2=80=99s investme=
nt committee, there was value in getting started while she remained at Enco=
mpass and could =E2=80=98access people and relationships.=E2=80=99 So they =
did.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

The judge also forged a creative remedy for EHC, against VitalCaring. First=
, she awarded EHC $1.62 million in =E2=80=9Cmitigation
damages=E2=80=9D and an unspecified amount of attorneys fees =E2=80=9Cbased=
 on the defendants=E2=80=99 bad faith efforts to conceal their misdeeds, wh=
ich included falsifying records, deleting evidence, and =E2=80=98manipulati=
ng communications through lawyers.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D She also created a =E2=
=80=9Cconstructive trust=E2=80=9D that would award EHC 43 percent of VitalC=
aring=E2=80=99s future profits as well as 43 percent of any future sale pro=
ceeds when, and if, VitalCaring is sold by the three partners.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

The problem Judge Will faced in crafting a remedy was that VitalCaring has =
basically flopped since the end of the pandemic, widely missing its earning=
s projections and remaining unprofitable. So, she resolved to reward EHC ou=
t of the future profits of VitalCaring while also incentivizing the three p=
artners to generate those profits and/or sale proceeds by letting them keep=
 the other 57 percent. She also directed that a trustee be appointed to ove=
rsee the
=E2=80=9Cconstructive trust=E2=80=9D to make sure that everyone behaves, an=
d that Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria don=E2=80=99t figure out how to game th=
e court=E2=80=99s judgment.

=C2=A0

EHC lauded the court ruling. It was a clever solution to a complex problem,=
 but also one, potentially, with real teeth. The judge, in effect, gave the=
 plaintiff 43 percent of the equity of the defendant=E2=80=99s company. I=
=E2=80=99ve never heard of such a thing before. It=E2=80=99s an outcome tha=
t could
probably only occur in an industry with so much money sloshing around, and =
one with a pervasive promiscuity for pushing boundaries=E2=80=94one where t=
he line between deception and creativity can be extremely blurry. EHC and i=
ts affiliates =E2=80=9Claunched the litigation to protect the interests of =
their stockholders in the wake of the illegal and outrageous conduct of Ant=
hony and the other former officers,=E2=80=9D it wrote in a press release th=
at has received little public attention, just like this entire sorry saga.
(Neither Nautic nor Vistria responded to my requests for comment on Judge W=
ill=E2=80=99s ruling.)

=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Private equity has become such an astoundi=
ngly lucrative
business that I guess it is no surprise that the temptation of ever greater=
 fortune sometimes leads its practitioners astray. On December 4, the Delaw=
are Court of Chancery=E2=80=99s Judge <strong>Lori Will</strong> wrote a no=
tably damning opinion involving VitalCaring Group, which had been created b=
y two mid-market P.E. firms=E2=80=94The Vistria Group, based in Chicago, an=
d Nautic Partners, based in Providence=E2=80=94to buy companies in the home=
 healthcare industry. According to the judge, Vistria and Nautic aided and
abetted the =E2=80=9Cegregious breaches of the duty of loyalty=E2=80=9D owe=
d by three executives that the firms had poached from their former employer=
, Encompass Health Corporation.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;"=
>=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">I=E2=80=99ve read many =
corporate legal opinions over the decades, but I can=E2=80=99t remember one=
 as scathing as Judge Will=E2=80=99s 114-page barn burner of a ruling in a =
lawsuit brought by Encompass against Vistria and its senior partner <strong=
>David
Schuppan</strong>, and Nautic and its managing director, <strong>Christophe=
r Corey</strong>, among others. =E2=80=9CDelaware law demands that corporat=
e officers act with the utmost loyalty to the entity they serve,=E2=80=9D s=
he wrote. =E2=80=9CThey must avoid advantaging themselves at the corporatio=
n=E2=80=99s expense. They cannot compete with the corporation or divert cor=
porate opportunities from it without its consent. And they must undertake g=
ood faith efforts to advance the corporation=E2=80=99s best interests. The =
former officers
at issue here lost sight of this enduring duty.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;=
">Of course, competition between private equity firms has never been so int=
ense. Over the past three decades, the number of firms competing for deals =
has risen exponentially=E2=80=94along with the <em>trillions</em> of dollar=
s they collectively manage. We=E2=80=99ve all heard of the biggies=E2=80=94=
Blackstone, Carlyle, Apollo (the subject of my new book), KKR, and TPG (an =
investor in
Puck), among others ranking in the top 50. According to IBISWorld, however,=
 there are now more than <em>11,000</em> private equity and hedge fund firm=
s=E2=80=94<em>alts</em>, in the lingua franca of modern finance=E2=80=94ope=
rating in the United States alone. According to ProPublica, they collective=
ly manage more than $6 trillion in assets=E2=80=94up from less than $600 bi=
llion in 2000.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p>=
<p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Until 2004, when I left
JPMorgan Chase, I worked as a banker to private equity firms when the indus=
try was still in its relative infancy. I=E2=80=99ve been reporting and writ=
ing about these firms ever since. Before reading Judge Will=E2=80=99s damni=
ng ruling, I=E2=80=99d never heard of either Vistria or Nautic, which goes =
to show how flooded the zone has become. The only dealmaker I recognized at=
 either firm was <strong>Deval Patrick</strong>, the former good-guy govern=
or of Massachusetts, who worked at Bain Capital before he joined Vistria.
But the backstory of the two firms=E2=80=99 legal troubles=E2=80=94the psyc=
hodrama of building and selling companies, and the ego-fueled attempts to b=
uy them back=E2=80=94will be familiar to many of my readers.</p><p style=3D=
"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:=
0px;">The sorry saga began when <strong>April Anthony</strong>, the founder=
 and C.E.O. of what was then called Encompass Home Health & Hospice, a =
home healthcare company, =E2=80=9Cbecame disillusioned after her business w=
as bought by a
large public healthcare company=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94the Birmingham, Alabama-ba=
sed HealthSouth=E2=80=94in 2014. After Anthony=E2=80=99s initial attempts t=
o buy back Encompass failed, she recruited two of her colleagues=E2=80=94pr=
esident <strong>Luke</strong> <strong>James</strong> and C.F.O. <strong>Chr=
is</strong> <strong>Walker</strong>=E2=80=94and =E2=80=9Csecretly partnered=
=E2=80=9D with Vistria and Nautic to =E2=80=9Cforge another path=E2=80=9D t=
hat got them all in a whole heap of trouble. Together, the trio formed a ne=
w entity=E2=80=94what became VitalCaring=E2=80=94to buy companies that
<em>competed</em> with Encompass, all while mostly still <em>working for</e=
m> Encompass, in violation of their contractual obligations to the company.=
 Per the ruling, =E2=80=9CAnthony and her co-venturers identified three acq=
uisition targets to form the base of their enterprise. Their scheme was kep=
t from Encompass and its private equity investors. They took opportunities,=
 resources, and information belonging to Encompass to set themselves up for=
 success. After the new company was formed, Anthony
induced more Encompass employees to join her. Anthony=E2=80=99s private equ=
ity partners were active participants in the fiduciaries=E2=80=99 misconduc=
t.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">According to Judge Will, this group =E2=80=
=9Cundertook stunning efforts to conceal their actions=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94inc=
luding exchanging documents through lawyers and on a golf course, and using=
 sophomoric code names like =E2=80=9CVoldemort,=E2=80=9D a veiled reference=
 to Anthony, since, you know, she could not
be named. A phony employee-recruitment process was contrived to set up Vita=
lCaring, with Anthony operating as C.E.O. despite her official ongoing obli=
gations at Encompass, referred to as EHC in the court documents. Meanwhile,=
 various records demonstrating her involvement were deleted or manipulated.=
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin:0;">The whole thing might have worked out just fine but for the fact t=
hat one EHC employee Anthony was trying to recruit
later changed her mind and told the new C.E.O. of EHC what was going on.</p=
></div>

pace:0pt;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-bottom:32px;padding-=
left:24px;padding-right:24px;text-align:center;width:100%;"><h3 style=3D"ma=
rgin:0;color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'PuckSerifDisplay',&=
#39;Georgia';font-size:24px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:normal;line-=
height:120%;text-align:center;margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;mso-line-height-=
alt:
28.799999999999997px;"><strong>Too Much Is Never Enough</strong></h3>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<table class=3D"c4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspaci=
ng=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspa=
ce:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-=
bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'In=
ter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;=
line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">It=E2=80=99s a little late for a holiday season=
 morality tale, but it=E2=80=99s
worth noting that no one in this saga was at risk of going hungry. Accordin=
g to a lavish 2020 <em>Forbes</em> profile, Anthony drives around town in a=
 plum-colored Bentley, a black Rolls-Royce Wraith, and a white McLaren, and=
 has ranked as high as No. 45 on the magazine=E2=80=99s list of America=E2=
=80=99s Richest Self-Made Women, tied with fellow Houston native <strong>Be=
yonc=C3=A9</strong>. Her entrepreneurial career began in the 1990s with Lib=
erty Health Services, a home healthcare business with about 25 employees
and 50 in-home patients. She sold that company for $40 million in 1996, and=
 in 1998 she took the $3 million she received in proceeds=E2=80=94and the l=
essons that she had learned in the salt mines of entrepreneurship=E2=80=94t=
o form Encompass Home Health & Hospice by rolling up some 17 struggling=
 Texas home healthcare providers. In 2004, she sold two-thirds of Encompass=
 to the buyout firm Cressey & Company at a valuation of $280 million. S=
he also created Homecare Homebase, a medical records software
company.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In 2014, Anthony demonstrated that she h=
ad mastered the dark arts of the private equity business. Along with Cresse=
y, she sold Encompass for $750 million to the HealthSouth Corporation, whic=
h later changed its name to Encompass Health Corporation. Anthony received =
$70 million in proceeds from that sale, $53 million of which she rolled int=
o the equity of the new company. She remained C.E.O.
of the home health and hospice division of Encompass Health Corporation.</p=
><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;ma=
rgin-bottom:0px;">With Anthony at the helm and James as president, Encompas=
s=E2=80=99s home health and hospice division grew its revenue from $400 mil=
lion in 2014 to $1.1 billion in 2021, receiving a big boost from the demand=
 for its services during the pandemic. It also made some 36 acquisitions, t=
otaling $750 million. In the spring of 2020, Anthony and James
sold the rest of the equity they=E2=80=99d rolled over into Encompass, for =
$370 million and $45 million, respectively. That year, they told <em>Forbes=
</em> they were =E2=80=9Cready to move on=E2=80=9D from Encompass Health an=
d wanted to focus on end-of-life care.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin=
-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">About a mo=
nth later, however, Christopher Corey, at Nautic, approached Anthony, whom =
he had known for more than a decade, and suggested they work together again=
=2E
According to Judge Will=E2=80=99s ruling, Anthony told Corey that she wante=
d to leave EHC and start a new company. But <em>first</em>, according to th=
e document, she wanted to see if she could buy her old company=E2=80=94the =
home and hospice care division=E2=80=94back from Encompass.=C2=A0</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Anthony=
 and Corey worked together to model how the acquisition might work. In Sept=
ember 2020, Anthony also contacted David Schuppan, a former Bear Stearns
banker whom she knew from when he worked at Cressey & Company, and who =
was now a senior partner at Vistria and co-head of its healthcare business.=
 Anthony introduced Corey to Schuppan, and the two buyout firms agreed to w=
ork together with her on the project. Later that month, Anthony approached =
the C.E.O. of Encompass Health with her proposal.=C2=A0</p></div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>

e:0pt;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-bottom:32px;padding-lef=
t:24px;padding-right:24px;text-align:center;width:100%;"><h3 style=3D"margi=
n:0;color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'PuckSerifDisplay',&#39=
;Georgia';font-size:24px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:normal;line-hei=
ght:120%;text-align:center;margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;mso-line-height-alt=
:
28.799999999999997px;"><strong>What Could Possibly Go Wrong?</strong></h3>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<table class=3D"c4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspaci=
ng=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspa=
ce:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-=
bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'In=
ter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;=
line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In the midst of those discussions, Anthony also=
 made a bid on
behalf of Encompass to acquire a new entity, the home health and hospice se=
rvices business of a company called Brookdale, for between $350 and $400 mi=
llion. She also told Nautic and Vistria about the Brookdale bid without tel=
ling her bosses at Encompass that she had shared that information with her =
two buyout firm partners.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;"=
>=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Meanwhile, Nautic and V=
istria began studying Brookdale=E2=80=99s business. In
December 2020, Nautic submitted its own bid for the Brookdale division, whi=
le Vistria approached Encompass about bidding jointly for the same property=
=2E =E2=80=9CCorey sent a Nautic dec=
k to Anthony=E2=80=99s husband=E2=80=99s=20=
personal email modeling a combined leveraged buyout of Encompass Home Healt=
h and an acquisition of Brookdale=E2=80=99s home health business,=E2=80=9D =
the judge wrote. =E2=80=9CThe deck was then forwarded to Walker=E2=80=99s p=
ersonal email, with the subject line changed to =E2=80=98From Mark Anthony=
=E2=80=99 (Anthony=E2=80=99s husband). Anthony sought
input from Walker and James to refine the projections using Encompass=E2=80=
=99s information.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=
=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Encompass ended up passing =
on the Brookdale property. That=E2=80=99s when Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria=
 submitted a non-binding bid to buy Anthony=E2=80=99s old business back fro=
m Encompass for $3.6 billion. =E2=80=9CThe proposal cautioned that =E2=80=
=98April [was] not likely to support an alternative transaction involving t=
he sale of [Encompass Home
Health] to another third party or a public offering of the [Encompass Home =
Health] business in a spin-out transaction,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D Judge Will wr=
ote. =E2=80=9CAnthony planned to resign if EHC rejected it. EHC was surpris=
ed. Before receiving the proposal, EHC had no idea that Anthony was partner=
ing with private equity firms on a transaction involving Encompass Home Hea=
lth.=E2=80=9D EHC never responded to the buyout proposal, and it expired af=
ter 30 days.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin:
0;margin-bottom:0px;">In January 2021, Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria tried a=
nother tack. They decided to set up a new company, in which they would all =
invest, to buy other home and hospice care companies to compete with Encomp=
ass=E2=80=94even though Anthony remained the C.E.O. of a division of Encomp=
ass. That=E2=80=99s a Wall Street no-no.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bot=
tom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">They hired leg=
al advisors at Ropes & Gray, the august Boston firm, and engaged
Harris Williams, the middle-market investment bank, to help find acquisitio=
n targets for the new company. The group quickly identified three prospects=
: Homecare Holdings, which Harris Williams was selling; Vital Health Care; =
and Kare-In-Home. Anthony was heavily involved in the creation of the new c=
ompany, according to Judge Will, and the negotiation of the acquisition tar=
gets while still at EHC. =E2=80=9CShe had frequent Zoom meetings, phone cal=
ls, and in-person meetings with Nautic, Vistria, and
representatives from the target companies,=E2=80=9D the judge wrote. =E2=80=
=9CReferences to her involvement were scrubbed or replaced with sly codenam=
es.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0=
</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Anthony mentioned none of this to her bosses at =
Encompass. =E2=80=9CIt was not lost on Nautic and Vistria that Anthony=E2=
=80=99s fiduciary duties to Encompass created risks to their plans,=E2=80=
=9D the judge wrote. =E2=80=9CThey were also concerned about contractual re=
strictive covenants that would continue
to bind Anthony after her employment ended. They asked Ropes for advice on =
navigating these issues.=E2=80=9D Ropes provided the advice, which, accordi=
ng to Judge Will, the defendants ignored.=C2=A0</p></div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<table class=3D"g" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacin=
g=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspac=
e:0pt;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-bottom:32px;text-align:=
center;width:100%;"><h3 style=3D"margin:0;color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-=
family:'PuckSerifDisplay','Georgia';font-size:24px;font-wei=
ght:400;letter-spacing:normal;line-height:120%;text-align:center;margin-top=
:0;margin-bottom:0;mso-line-height-alt:28.799999999999997px;"><strong>43 Pe=
rcent</strong>
</h3>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<table class=3D"c4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspaci=
ng=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspa=
ce:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D"r" style=3D"padding-=
bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:ltr;font-family:'In=
ter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:400;letter-spacing:0px;=
line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"m=
argin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">On March 18, 2021, Anthony informed Encompass t=
hat she would be
resigning, effective June 18. She said that she planned to spend time at he=
r homes in Cabo and Idaho, and playing golf. =E2=80=9CThat was false,=E2=80=
=9D the judge wrote. =E2=80=9CAnthony instead continued her efforts to form=
 a home healthcare company with Nautic and Vistria. Anthony=E2=80=99s effor=
ts included recruiting certain Encompass employees for the venture.=E2=80=
=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D=
"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">On July 30, 2021, the new company bought Home=
care Holdings for
$192 million. In August 2021, it bought Vital for $106 million. In December=
 2021, it bought Kare for $110 million. Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria each i=
nvested $87 million for a one-third stake in VitalCaring Group.</p><p style=
=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bott=
om:0px;">In September 2021, after an EHC employee told the new EHC C.E.O. a=
bout Anthony=E2=80=99s recruitment efforts, EHC sent cease and desist lette=
rs to Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria, among others. The next
month, EHC sued Anthony in Texas state court for breaching the restrictive =
covenants in her EHC contract. While the Texas court ruled that Anthony had=
 breached some of the covenants, it awarded no damages to EHC but required =
Anthony to comply with her contractual covenants until her contract expired=
=2E The court found that Nautic and=20=
Vistria =E2=80=9Chad tried to conceal Ant=
hony=E2=80=99s involvement.=E2=80=9D Each side paid their own legal fees. A=
nthony became the C.E.O. of VitalCaring in August 2022, after the covenant
restrictions lapsed.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=
=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">A month later, EHC and seve=
ral affiliates sued Nautic, Vistria, Corey, Schuppan, and Walker in Delawar=
e court, alleging six counts of aiding and abetting Anthony in breaches of =
her fiduciary duty to EHC, among other charges. A seven-day trial was held =
in December 2023. In her ruling a year later, Judge Will has thrown pretty =
much the whole book at Nautic and Vistria. According to
the Encompass press release, =E2=80=9CAfter considering =E2=80=98the damnin=
g record presented at trial,=E2=80=99 the Court found that, while employed =
by Encompass Health, Anthony, James, and Walker usurped acquisition opportu=
nities falling within Encompass Health=E2=80=99s line of business, used Enc=
ompass Health=E2=80=99s confidential information, and swayed key Encompass =
Health employees to join them with the promise of equity in the home health=
 and hospice competitor that Anthony now heads.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">
=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Judge Will was more than=
 a little peeved. =E2=80=9CThe evidence establishes that Anthony and James =
strove to benefit themselves at Encompass=E2=80=99s expense,=E2=80=9D she w=
rote. =E2=80=9CThey did so willfully, using code names and secretly exchang=
ing diligence materials to hide their misconduct. This deception is unexcus=
ed by the defendants=E2=80=99 post hoc justifications.=E2=80=9D She added, =
=E2=80=9CDisillusioned with EHC, Anthony set out to =E2=80=98get [her] baby=
 back.=E2=80=99 She partnered with Nautic and
Vistria and brought along James and Walker. When her plan failed, she turne=
d her focus to creating a competitor. As she told Nautic=E2=80=99s investme=
nt committee, there was value in getting started while she remained at Enco=
mpass and could =E2=80=98access people and relationships.=E2=80=99 So they =
did.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The judge also forged a creative remedy f=
or EHC, against VitalCaring. First, she awarded EHC $1.62 million in =E2=80=
=9Cmitigation
damages=E2=80=9D and an unspecified amount of attorneys fees =E2=80=9Cbased=
 on the defendants=E2=80=99 bad faith efforts to conceal their misdeeds, wh=
ich included falsifying records, deleting evidence, and =E2=80=98manipulati=
ng communications through lawyers.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D She also created a =E2=
=80=9Cconstructive trust=E2=80=9D that would award EHC 43 percent of VitalC=
aring=E2=80=99s future profits as well as 43 percent of any future sale pro=
ceeds when, and if, VitalCaring is sold by the three partners.=C2=A0</p><p =
style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">The problem Judge Will faced in cr=
afting a remedy was that VitalCaring has basically flopped since the end of=
 the pandemic, widely missing its earnings projections and remaining unprof=
itable. So, she resolved to reward EHC out of the future profits of VitalCa=
ring while also incentivizing the three partners to generate those profits =
and/or sale proceeds by letting them keep the other 57 percent. She also di=
rected that a trustee be appointed to oversee the
=E2=80=9Cconstructive trust=E2=80=9D to make sure that everyone behaves, an=
d that Anthony, Nautic, and Vistria don=E2=80=99t figure out how to game th=
e court=E2=80=99s judgment.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=
=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">EHC lauded the court ruling. It was a clever =
solution to a complex problem, but also one, potentially, with real teeth. =
The judge, in effect, gave the plaintiff 43 percent of the equity of the de=
fendant=E2=80=99s company. I=E2=80=99ve never heard of such a thing before.=
 It=E2=80=99s an outcome that could
probably only occur in an industry with so much money sloshing around, and =
one with a pervasive promiscuity for pushing boundaries=E2=80=94one where t=
he line between deception and creativity can be extremely blurry. EHC and i=
ts affiliates =E2=80=9Claunched the litigation to protect the interests of =
their stockholders in the wake of the illegal and outrageous conduct of Ant=
hony and the other former officers,=E2=80=9D it wrote in a press release th=
at has received little public attention, just like this entire sorry saga.
(Neither Nautic nor Vistria responded to my requests for comment on Judge W=
ill=E2=80=99s ruling.)</p></div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
